summary: A maturity model is a tool for assessing current effectiveness and prioritizing what capabilities to learn next, structured as sequential levels of increasing competence. Fowler argues that the true value lies not in determining your current level, but in identifying what skills to acquire to improve. While maturity models have gained a bad reputation in software circles—largely due to the Capability Maturity Model's association with document-heavy processes and corrupted certification schemes—they can still be useful when applied properly. The key danger is using levels to compare or judge groups rather than to guide learning, which ruins the model's informational value. Any maturity model is a simplification that may not fit all contexts, and users should carefully evaluate fitness before applying one.
key_points: The true outcome of a maturity model assessment is the list of things to improve, not the level itself
Certification and competitive comparison corrupt maturity models by incentivizing gaming rather than genuine improvement
Maturity models may conflate level of effectiveness with optimal learning path, suggesting easier-to-harder progression rather than the best sequence for learning
Like any model, maturity models are simplifications that may not fit all contexts and require careful evaluation before use
Assessment results should never be publicized outside the group being assessed to avoid corrupting the metric
money_quotes: The true outcome of a maturity model assessment isn't what level you are but the list of things you need to work on to improve.
Any maturity model, like any model, is a simplification: wrong but hopefully useful.
Using a maturity model to say one group is better than another is a classic example of ruining an informational metric by incentivizing it.
It may be that this tendency to compare levels to judge worth is a fundamentally destructive feature of a maturity model, one that will always undermine any positive value that comes from it.
The maturity model conflates level of effectiveness with learning path.
Too often, people don't put enough care in evaluating the fitness of a model for a situation before they leap to using it.
key_insight: Maturity models are valuable for prioritizing learning, not for ranking or certification—the moment levels become competitive metrics, their core purpose is corrupted.
